title: CS Theory Equations — ELC Boundaries, Complexity Functions, Information Theory, Neural Networks

superBEST_v5_costs.add: 2n  (universal — ADD-T1: sign-definiteness no longer drives cost)
superBEST_v5_costs.mul: 2n  (positive inputs; same in v5 for general inputs)

ELC_definition.formal: ELC(R) = exp-log closure over the reals = smallest set containing R closed under exp and ln and the four arithmetic operations
ELC_definition.characterization: Analytic functions — smooth, no jumps, no discontinuities, no integer-valued output for real-valued input
ELC_definition.key_property: Every function in ELC(R) is analytic (infinitely differentiable, equals its Taylor series locally). Discontinuous or integer-valued functions are outside ELC(R) by this criterion.

outside_types.type_A: Computable but not ELC — e.g., cos(x) (infinitely many zeros on R, T01 criterion), ceil(x), floor(x), x mod p. These can be computed by finite algorithms but lie outside ELC(R).
outside_types.type_B: Uncomputable entirely — e.g., Kolmogorov complexity K(x). These are outside ALL computable functions, not merely outside ELC. A strictly stronger form of 'outside'.

section_1_complexity_functions.title: Complexity Functions (the functions inside Big-O notation)
section_1_complexity_functions.note: These are functions of n used to express algorithm complexity. The question is whether f(n), as a real-valued function, lies inside ELC(R).

functions.name: n * ln(n)

steps.note: 1n — ln is a primitive ELC operation
2n — product of two positive reals for n > 0

functions.justification: ln(n) is the canonical ELC primitive (1n). Multiplication by n adds 2n. Product of analytic functions is analytic. Appears in merge sort, heap sort, FFT complexity.

functions.name: n^2

steps.note: 3n — pow is a single call, degree-independent in v5

functions.justification: n^2 = exp(2*ln(n)) for n > 0. SuperBEST v5 pow costs 3n regardless of exponent. Polynomial functions are ELC-native. Appears in bubble sort, insertion sort, matrix multiplication (naive).
functions.alt_decomposition: exp(2*ln(n)) = ln(1n) + mul(2n) + exp(1n) = 4n via explicit ELC path; but pow(n,2) at 3n is cheaper per v5 rules

functions.name: 2^n

steps.note: 3n — pow is constant-cost per call in v5; here base is constant 2

functions.justification: 2^n = exp(n * ln(2)) for all real n. ln(2) is a numeric constant (free). So: mul(n, ln2) = 2n + exp(1n) = 3n via explicit ELC path — same as direct pow. Exponential growth is the defining ELC pattern. Appears in subset enumeration, brute-force search.
functions.domain: all real n (but algorithm complexity: n >= 0)
functions.alt_decomposition: exp(n * ln(2)): ln(2) is constant (0n), mul(n, ln2) = 2n, exp = 1n → total 3n (matches pow cost)

functions.name: n! (factorial)

exact_status.reason: n! for integer n requires the Gamma function: n! = Gamma(n+1). The Gamma function Gamma(z) has poles at z = 0, -1, -2, ... (negative integers). While Gamma is meromorphic on C and analytic on R⁺, it is NOT in ELC(R) because it cannot be expressed as a finite composition of exp, ln, and arithmetic — it requires an improper integral definition. The exact integer factorial is also a discrete function when restricted to N.
exact_status.flag: OUTSIDE ELC(R) exactly. The Gamma function is a transcendental function beyond ELC.

stirling_approximation.flag: THIS IS AN APPROXIMATION. Valid asymptotically (n → ∞) but not exact for any finite n.

step_1_sqrt_2pi_n.desc: sqrt(2*pi*n): 2*pi is a constant, so this is sqrt(const * n) = sqrt(n) * sqrt(2*pi)

ops.note: 2n — constant * n; 2*pi treated as numeric constant
2n — sqrt is a 2n primitive in v5

stirling_approximation.accuracy: Relative error O(1/n) — Stirling's approximation improves as n grows. For n=10: 3628800 exact vs 3598696 Stirling (0.83% error). For n=100: error < 0.08%.

functions.summary: Exact n! is OUTSIDE ELC(R). Stirling approximation of n! is INSIDE ELC(R) at 12n total cost, but is an approximation. These are fundamentally different: one is a mathematical identity, the other is an asymptotic approximation.
functions.use_cases: n! appears in permutations, combinatorics, brute-force TSP. Stirling is used in statistical mechanics, asymptotic analysis, information theory (log n!).

section_2_discrete_outside_ELC.title: Discrete Functions — OUTSIDE ELC(R)
section_2_discrete_outside_ELC.central_reason: ELC(R) consists exclusively of analytic functions: functions that are smooth (infinitely differentiable), have no jumps, no integer-valued outputs for real inputs, and equal their local Taylor series. Any function that is discontinuous, piecewise-constant, or integer-valued on a real domain violates analyticity and is therefore outside ELC(R).

functions.name: Ceiling function
functions.reason: ceil(x) is piecewise constant with jump discontinuities at every integer. It has a derivative of 0 almost everywhere but is not differentiable at integers (left limit ≠ right limit). Analytic functions cannot have jump discontinuities. Any analytic function equal to ceil(x) on an interval would have to be a polynomial — but no polynomial matches ceil(x).
functions.formal: For any n in Z: lim_{x→n⁻} ceil(x) = n-1, but lim_{x→n⁺} ceil(x) = n. Jump of 1 at every integer. Not differentiable, hence not analytic.

functions.name: Floor function
functions.reason: Same argument as ceil. floor(x) = ceil(x) - 1 (shifted by 1), so shares all discontinuities. Additionally, floor is the integer part function — it maps reals to integers, which is structurally incompatible with analytic real-to-real functions.
functions.formal: For any n in Z: lim_{x→n⁻} floor(x) = n-1, lim_{x→n⁺} floor(x) = n. Jump of 1 at every integer downward.

functions.name: Modular arithmetic
functions.formula: x mod p = x - p * floor(x/p)
functions.reason: x mod p is a sawtooth function — periodic with period p, rising linearly then dropping discontinuously to 0 at each multiple of p. The discontinuous drops make it non-analytic. Also: x mod p involves floor(x/p) which is itself outside ELC (D2 above). Even without floor, the sawtooth shape has countably many discontinuities.
functions.formal: At x = k*p for integer k: lim_{x→(kp)⁻} (x mod p) = p, but (kp mod p) = 0. Jump of p at every multiple of p.
functions.applications: Cryptography, hash functions, cyclic groups, modular exponentiation — all involve mod and are therefore outside real ELC.
functions.ELC_flag: OUTSIDE_ELC — sawtooth discontinuities at all multiples of p

functions.name: Kronecker delta
functions.reason: The Kronecker delta is a discrete, integer-valued function of discrete indices — it is not even a function of real variables in the standard sense. When extended to real arguments (as a piecewise function: 1 if x=y, 0 otherwise), it is discontinuous everywhere off the diagonal. No analytic function can equal 1 at all points x=y and 0 at all x≠y simultaneously.
functions.distinction_from_dirac: The Dirac delta δ(x) is a distribution, not a function — also outside ELC(R) for different reasons (it is not a proper function at all). The Kronecker delta is discrete; neither is in ELC(R).
functions.ELC_flag: OUTSIDE_ELC — discrete-valued, discontinuous, defined on discrete domain

characterization_insight.title: THE KEY INSIGHT: ELC(R) = continuous mathematics; discrete math is largely outside
characterization_insight.statement: ELC(R) is the domain of smooth, analytic, non-oscillatory, non-discrete real-valued functions. The boundary between inside and outside ELC(R) tracks the boundary between continuous mathematics and discrete mathematics.
inside: Rational functions (quotients of polynomials, via div)
Products and compositions of the above
Physical rate laws, growth models, decay models
Sigmoid, softplus, and other smooth neural network activations
outside: All integer-valued functions of real arguments (floor, ceil, round, sign when defined piecewise)
Combinatorial functions: n!, C(n,k), etc. (exact, for integer n)
Discrete probability mass functions (Kronecker delta, indicator functions)
Trigonometric functions on R (infinitely many zeros — T01 criterion)
Piecewise-defined functions with finitely many pieces: max(0,x) = ReLU, abs(x)
Kolmogorov complexity K(x) — uncomputable entirely (Type B outside)
characterization_insight.nuance: Some functions appear discrete but have ELC-amenable approximations: Stirling for n!, softplus for ReLU. The approximation is inside ELC; the exact function is outside. This distinction matters for algorithmic analysis.

section_3_information_theory.title: Information Theory — SuperBEST v5 Update
section_3_information_theory.note: v5 key change: add=2n universally (ADD-T1). The add_gen vs add_pos distinction from v4 is eliminated. Shannon and KL divergence now have the same per-term structure (7N-2 for the neg-first strategy; 5N for the optimal direct-sum strategy in Shannon).
section_3_information_theory.v4_to_v5_summary: In v4, sign-definiteness drove costs: add_pos=3n (non-negative terms), add_gen=11n (mixed-sign terms). In v5, add=2n for ALL real x,y. This collapses the gap: KL divergence dropped from 16N-11 to 7N-2, matching Shannon entropy.

functions.name: Shannon Entropy
functions.cost_formula_v5_optimal: 5N  (direct-sum strategy: compute p_i*ln(p_i) for each i, sum, then negate — optimal for N >= 3)

direct_sum_strategy.note: Direct-sum is optimal in v5 for N >= 3 because the outer neg(2n) is paid once vs neg(2n) per-term in the neg-first strategy.

functions.name: Cross-Entropy Loss
functions.note: Identical structure to Shannon entropy — substitute q_i for p_i inside the ln. Zero ELC cost difference from Shannon. This is the standard ML training loss (cross-entropy loss for classification).
functions.structural_isomorphism: H(P,Q) and H(P,P) = H(P) have identical EML cost. The ML loss function IS the information-theoretic cross-entropy, same formula, same cost.

functions.name: KL Divergence

per_term_breakdown.note: 2n — term can be positive or negative

functions.note_v5_parity: v5 PARITY FINDING: KL divergence now costs the SAME as Shannon entropy (7N-2). In v4, KL was 16N-11 (2.3x Shannon) because mixed-sign terms required add_gen=11n. In v5, add=2n for all, so KL and Shannon are structurally equivalent. The entire v4 cost gap was an artifact of sign-definiteness accounting.
functions.grand_total: 4N + 2N-2 = 6N-2... CORRECTION: per_term = div(1)+ln(1)+mul(2) = 4n, so 4N + 2(N-1) = 6N-2. But cross-checking with INFO-1 session: div in INFO-1 was counted at 2n. See note.
functions.div_cost_note: In SuperBEST v5: div=1n per the task specification. In INFO-1 file, div was listed as 2n in the operator table. Using task-specified v5: div=1n. Per-term cost: div(1n)+ln(1n)+mul(2n) = 4n. Total: 4N + 2(N-1) = 6N-2.

section_3_information_theory.v5_parity_summary: All three core information-theoretic loss functions (Shannon, cross-entropy, KL divergence) are now within the same order of cost in v5. The v4 KL penalty is gone. This is a structural consequence of ADD-T1: sign-definiteness no longer drives cost differences.

section_4_neural_network_operations.title: Neural Network Operations — ELC Status and Costs

functions.name: Sigmoid activation

steps.note: 2n — 1 + exp(-x); both terms positive; add=2n v5

functions.note_from_task: Task specifies 7n. Recount: neg(2) + exp(1) + add(2) + recip(1) = 6n. The discrepancy may come from whether div(1/(1+exp(-x))) is counted as div(1n) or recip(1n) + whether neg is included. Using recip=1n and neg=2n: total = 6n. Using div instead of recip: neg(2)+exp(1)+add(2)+div(1) = 6n. Either way: 6n.
functions.clarification: If 1 in the numerator is written as a literal (not a variable), it is a constant (0n). If it is treated as the constant function f=1 that must be evaluated: 0n anyway. Total: 2+1+2+1 = 6n.
functions.ELC_justification: sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x}) is a composition of neg, exp, add, and recip — all ELC operations. sigma is smooth (infinitely differentiable), monotone, asymptotes to 0 and 1, no discontinuities. Classic ELC function.

functions.name: Softmax with temperature scaling

temperature_preprocessing.note: div=1n in v5; T is a positive constant parameter

functions.ELC_justification: Softmax is a composition of div, exp, add, and div — all ELC operations. The temperature parameter T > 0 is a free parameter (0 cost). Output is a probability simplex; function is smooth everywhere.

functions.name: ReLU activation
functions.reason: ReLU is piecewise linear with a kink at x=0: it equals 0 for x < 0 and x for x > 0. At x=0, the left derivative is 0 and the right derivative is 1 — so ReLU is not differentiable at x=0. A function must be infinitely differentiable (analytic) to be in ELC(R). ReLU fails this at x=0.
functions.ELC_flag: OUTSIDE_ELC — not differentiable at x=0, hence not analytic

smooth_approximation.name: Softplus
smooth_approximation.note: Softplus converges to ReLU as the scale parameter increases: softplus(x/beta)/beta → ReLU(x) as beta→0. It is a smooth, differentiable approximation INSIDE ELC(R) at 4n total cost.

functions.name: Learning rate schedule — Constant
functions.note: lr_0 is a constant parameter. Zero computational nodes needed. Trivially inside ELC (constants are ELC).

functions.name: Learning rate schedule — Exponential decay

steps.note: 2n — alpha is a constant parameter, t is the time variable
2n — scale by initial learning rate (lr_0 is constant)

functions.note_from_task: Task gives 1+2+2=5n decomposition. Recount with full breakdown: mul(2n) + neg(2n) + exp(1n) + mul(2n) = 7n. If alpha*t treats alpha as a free constant (0n) and t as the variable, then: neg(alpha*t) requires treating alpha*t as a subexpression. If we fold alpha into a constant multiplier: neg(t) takes 2n, then mul(alpha, neg_t) = 2n, then exp = 1n, then mul(lr0, exp) = 2n → 7n. Alternatively if neg is skipped by absorbing the sign into -alpha: mul(-alpha, t) = 2n (with -alpha as constant) + exp(1n) + mul(lr0, exp) = 2n → 5n. The 5n reading from the task is likely using this constant-absorption strategy.

task_decomposition_5n.reading: mul(-alpha, t) where -alpha is a single precomputed constant (0n)
task_decomposition_5n.note: 5n is correct if -alpha is treated as a single free constant (0n cost). Both 5n and 7n are valid depending on whether neg is counted as a separate node.

functions.ELC_justification: Exponential decay is a composition of linear scaling, negation, exp, and scaling — all ELC operations. Output is smooth, positive, monotone decreasing. Classic ELC function.

functions.name: Learning rate schedule — Cosine annealing
functions.formula: lr(t) = lr_min + 0.5 * (lr_max - lr_min) * (1 + cos(pi * t / T_max))
functions.reason: Cosine annealing requires cos(pi * t / T_max). The cosine function is OUTSIDE real ELC(R) by the T01 criterion: cos(x) has infinitely many zeros on R (at x = pi/2 + k*pi for all integers k). An ELC(R) function has at most finitely many zeros. Therefore cos is outside ELC(R), and any formula requiring cos is outside ELC(R).
functions.ELC_flag: OUTSIDE_ELC — cos is outside real ELC (T01: infinitely many zeros)
functions.flag: COSINE ANNEALING IS OUTSIDE ELC(R). Flag this when using it in ELC-constrained contexts.
functions.ELC_alternative: Use exponential decay (NN5, 5n) as an ELC-amenable alternative to cosine annealing for smooth learning rate reduction.

type_A.label: Computable but not ELC
type_A.description: Functions that can be computed by finite algorithms (Turing machines, programs) but cannot be expressed as a finite composition of exp, ln, and arithmetic operations. They are 'outside ELC' in the complexity-theoretic sense but remain within the computability-theoretic boundary.

examples.reason: Infinitely many zeros on R (T01 criterion). Can be computed by Taylor series or CORDIC algorithm. Computable but not ELC.
Same as cos. Computable, not ELC.
Computable by finite comparison and rounding. Discontinuous, not analytic, not ELC.
Computable by division and subtraction. Sawtooth discontinuities, not ELC.
Computable for integer n by multiplication loop. Requires Gamma function in general real setting. Not ELC.
Computable by comparison. Discrete-valued, not ELC.
Computable by if-then. Not differentiable at 0, not analytic, not ELC.

type_B.label: Uncomputable entirely — outside all computable functions
type_B.description: Functions that CANNOT be computed by any algorithm — they are not just outside ELC but outside the entire class of computable functions. This is a fundamentally stronger form of 'outside'. Kolmogorov complexity is the canonical example in CS theory.

examples.definition: K(x) = length of shortest program that outputs x on a fixed universal Turing machine
examples.proof_sketch: If K were computable, one could solve the halting problem: enumerate all strings by K-value, which would require deciding if programs halt. By reduction from the halting problem, K is uncomputable.
examples.type: Type B — outside ALL computable functions, not merely outside ELC
examples.importance: K(x) is a mathematically well-defined function but no algorithm can compute it. The set of values {K(x) : x in Sigma*} is computably enumerable (c.e.) but not decidable.
examples.approximation: K(x) can be APPROXIMATED from above: compress x with various compressors and take the best result. But the true K(x) is incomputable.

examples.definition: H(p,x) = 1 if program p halts on input x, else 0
Sigma(n) = maximum number of 1s written by any halting n-state Turing machine
examples.type: Type B — grows faster than any computable function

type_B.key_point: Type B functions are OUTSIDE ELC for a completely different reason than Type A. Type A functions fail the analyticity/closure criterion. Type B functions fail computability itself — no amount of exp/ln/arithmetic would help because no finite algorithm of any kind can compute them.

hierarchy.title: Hierarchy of Outside-ness
hierarchy.containment: ELC(R) ⊂ Computable ⊂ Total functions ⊂ Partial functions. Kolmogorov complexity K is not even a total computable function (no Turing machine computes it for all inputs).

rows.name: Shannon entropy
rows.flag: v5: add=2n; optimal strategy shifts to direct-sum

rows.name: KL divergence
rows.flag: v5 PARITY: now equals Shannon cost order

rows.name: Cosine annealing LR
rows.flag: OUTSIDE — cos is outside real ELC (T01)

discrete_continuous_boundary.title: The ELC Boundary: Continuous vs Discrete Mathematics
discrete_continuous_boundary.insight: ELC(R) draws a precise line between continuous and discrete mathematics. This is not a coincidence — it reflects the definition: ELC(R) is closed under operations (exp, ln, +, -, *, /) that preserve analyticity. Analyticity requires smoothness everywhere, which rules out all discrete constructs.

continuous_mathematics_inside_ELC.description: Real analysis, calculus, smooth dynamics, exponential/power/logarithmic growth, probability densities (continuous), information theory on continuous distributions

discrete_mathematics_outside_ELC.description: Combinatorics, number theory, modular arithmetic, integer sequences, discrete probability, graph theory functions, cryptographic primitives

hybrid_territory.description: Functions with both continuous and discrete aspects. Often the continuous part is inside ELC; the discrete part is outside.
examples: Stirling approximation: inside ELC (continuous approximation of discrete n!)
Softplus: inside ELC (continuous approximation of discrete-feeling ReLU)
Gamma function: outside ELC (continuous extension of n! — transcendental)
Poisson distribution PMF: p^k*e^{-lambda}/k! — the k! factor puts exact form outside ELC for integer k; the continuous envelope (replacing k! with Gamma) is also outside
